name: Creation Order
shortDescription: The Creation Order in Islam refers to the sequence of events and entities brought into existence by Allah. It highlights the divine plan and the hierarchy within creation.
description: In Islamic cosmology, the Creation Order is a complex and divinely orchestrated sequence. It begins with Allah, the ultimate creator, who existed before all things. The exact details of the creation order are debated among scholars, but a general understanding exists. Before the physical universe, it is believed that Allah created the Pen (al-Qalam) and the Tablet (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz), where all events are recorded. Then, Allah created water and the Throne ('Arsh). Subsequently, the angels, including Jibreel (Gabriel), were created from light. Jinn were created from smokeless fire before humans. Finally, Adam, the first human, was created from clay. The creation of the universe, including the heavens and the earth, followed. This order emphasizes the supremacy of Allah and the interconnectedness of all creation. The prophets, including Muhammad, Ibrahim, and Musa, play vital roles in guiding humanity within this divinely established order. Understanding the creation order is essential for comprehending the Islamic worldview and the relationship between humanity, the divine, and the universe.

location.cosmological: Existing beyond human comprehension, preceding the physical universe and encompassing all of creation. It is not a physical place, but a sequence of events and entities ordained by Allah.

sources.title: The Study Quran: A New Translation and Commentary
sources.author: Seyyed Hossein Nasr

worshipPractices.name: Remembrance of Allah's Power (Dhikr)
worshipPractices.description: Reflecting upon the creation order and Allah's role in it is a form of worship. Muslims engage in Dhikr, the remembrance of Allah, often reciting His names and attributes, acknowledging His power and wisdom in bringing the universe into existence. This is done through prayer, contemplation, and communal gatherings.

worshipPractices.name: Appreciation of Allah's Creation (Tafakkur)
worshipPractices.description: Observing the beauty and complexity of the universe and reflecting on its purpose is encouraged. Muslims are urged to engage in Tafakkur, thoughtful contemplation of Allah's creation, seeing it as a sign of His existence and a means of drawing closer to Him. This inspires awe and gratitude.

festivals.name: Eid al-Adha
festivals.timing: 10th day of Dhu al-Hijjah, the 12th month of the Islamic calendar
festivals.description: Commemorates Prophet Ibrahim's (Abraham's) willingness to sacrifice his son as an act of obedience to Allah. It involves the sacrifice of an animal and sharing the meat with family, friends, and the poor, reflecting the divine mercy and preservation of life within the creation order.

festivals.name: Laylat al-Qadr (Night of Power)
festivals.timing: One of the odd-numbered nights in the last ten days of Ramadan
festivals.description: Believed to be the night when the Quran was first revealed to Prophet Muhammad. Muslims spend the night in prayer and reflection, seeking forgiveness and blessings, as it is considered a night of immense spiritual significance and divine favor related to the unfolding of Allah's plan.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Islamic understanding of the Creation Order continues to inspire scientific inquiry within a faith-based framework, influencing approaches to environmental stewardship and ethical considerations in technology. It shapes artistic expressions through calligraphy, architecture, and literature that celebrate the beauty and harmony of the universe created by Allah.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Ginnungagap is the primordial void that existed before the creation of the Norse cosmos, similar to the pre-creation state before Allah's creative act in Islamic cosmology. Both represent a state of emptiness and potential from which creation emerges.

Chaos in Greek mythology is the formless void from which the first gods and the universe emerged, mirroring the initial state before Allah's creation order. Both represent a state of undifferentiated potential preceding the organized cosmos.

Brahman, the ultimate reality in Hinduism, is the source and ground of all existence, similar to Allah's role as the origin of the Creation Order. Both are considered the fundamental principle from which the universe emanates.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Represents the seven heavens, a structured part of the cosmic order established during creation.

associations.name: Stars
associations.significance: Stars are celestial bodies created to adorn the lowest heaven, indicating a structured and beautiful creation order.

associations.name: Light
associations.significance: Light symbolizes the divine illumination and guidance that initiated and sustains the creation order.

associations.name: Balance
associations.significance: The creation order is based on the principle of balance (Mizan) ensuring justice and harmony in the universe.

associations.name: Divine Decree (Qadar)
associations.significance: All events in creation occur according to Allah's pre-ordained knowledge and decree, reflecting a structured order.

associations.name: Angels
associations.significance: Angels are created beings who execute Allah's commands, maintaining and enforcing the divine creation order.

associations.name: Time
associations.significance: Time is a created dimension which provides a framework in which the creation order unfolds sequentially.

companions.name: Angels (Malaikah)
companions.description: Angels are created from light and are obedient servants of Allah, carrying out His commands and playing key roles in the execution of the Creation Order. They are instrumental in events like delivering messages to prophets and recording deeds.

companions.name: Jinn
companions.description: Created from smokeless fire, the Jinn were created before humans and given free will. They are a part of the created order, some are righteous, others are not, and their existence predates humanity's.

companions.name: Adam (Prophet Adam)
companions.description: Adam, the first human and prophet, is a key figure in the Creation Order, representing the culmination of Allah's creative power and the beginning of human history. He is the caretaker and protector of the Earth.

companions.name: The Pen (Qalam)
companions.description: The Pen is the first creation, used by Allah to inscribe the Preserved Tablet (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz) containing all that was, is, and will be. It is an instrument of divine decree and a crucial component in the Creation Order.

companions.name: The Preserved Tablet (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz)
companions.description: The Preserved Tablet is a record containing all that has been decreed by Allah, from the beginning of creation to its end. It serves as the blueprint and record of the entire Creation Order.

extendedContent.title: The Primordial Elements: Water, Smoke, and Light
extendedContent.content:
Islamic cosmology posits that before the formation of the Earth and heavens as we know them, there existed primordial elements: water, smoke, and light. The Quran alludes to this in multiple verses, such as Surah Hud (11:7), which mentions Allah's throne being upon water before the creation of the heavens and the earth. This primordial water is not the water we see today, but a foundational substance from which all things were derived. The 'smoke' mentioned in Surah Fussilat (41:11) is interpreted as a gaseous substance, a cosmic vapor from which the celestial bodies were formed. This 'smoke' is considered the raw material of the universe, awaiting divine command to take shape. The element of light is closely associated with the creation of angels and Jinn, beings composed of 'smokeless fire' or 'light,' signifying their spiritual and ethereal nature. The interplay of these three elements represents the initial stages of creation, a transition from nothingness to the potential for existence, all guided by Allah's divine will and power.

Scholars emphasize that these elements are not to be understood solely in a literal, physical sense. They also represent symbolic stages in the unfolding of creation. The water symbolizes the potential for life and sustenance, the smoke represents the formless, unmanifested matter, and the light represents the divine intellect and guidance that shapes the universe. Understanding these elements requires acknowledging the metaphorical language employed in religious texts to convey complex theological concepts.

 The arrangement of these elements also demonstrates the hierarchical nature of creation within the Islamic framework. The water, as the base upon which Allah's throne rested, symbolizes divine authority and control. The smoke, as the source of material creation, represents the foundation of the physical world. The light, as the essence of spiritual beings, signifies the higher realms of existence and the connection between the divine and the created. This intricate relationship highlights the interconnectedness of all things within the Islamic cosmology, all originating from and ultimately returning to Allah.

extendedContent.title: The Creation of the Angels and Jinn
extendedContent.content:
Before the creation of humankind, Islamic texts describe the creation of angels and Jinn. Angels, created from light (nur), are celestial beings who are devoted to Allah and carry out His commands. They are mentioned extensively throughout the Quran and Hadith, serving as messengers, guardians, and recorders of human deeds. Jibril (Gabriel) is perhaps the most well-known angel, responsible for delivering divine revelations to prophets, including Muhammad (peace be upon him). Angels are considered pure and obedient, incapable of disobedience or sin.

In contrast, Jinn were created from smokeless fire (marij min nar). They possess free will, unlike angels, and can choose to obey or disobey Allah. Some Jinn are righteous Muslims, while others are disbelievers or rebellious. Iblis (Satan) is the most prominent example of a Jinn who defied Allah's command to prostrate before Adam, leading to his expulsion from paradise and his eternal mission to mislead humanity. The existence of Jinn adds a layer of complexity to the Islamic worldview, introducing the concept of unseen beings who can interact with the human world.

The creation of angels and Jinn before humans highlights the divine plan and the preparation for the arrival of humanity. Angels were created to assist and guide humans, while Jinn were created with the capacity for both good and evil, presenting a test for human faith and obedience. This pre-human creation also emphasizes Allah's omnipotence and His ability to create a diverse range of beings with different natures and purposes. The narratives surrounding angels and Jinn serve as moral lessons and reminders of the spiritual dimensions of existence.

extendedContent.title: The Formation of the Heavens and the Earth
extendedContent.content:
The Quran describes the heavens and the earth as being initially joined together, then separated by Allah. Surah Al-Anbiya (21:30) states, "Have those who disbelieve not considered that the heavens and the earth were a joined entity, then We separated them?" This verse is often interpreted as a reference to the Big Bang theory, suggesting that the universe originated from a single point. The creation of the heavens involved the formation of seven layers, each with its own unique properties and inhabitants. These layers are described in varying levels of detail in the Quran and Hadith, often depicted as concentric spheres surrounding the earth.

The creation of the earth involved the formation of mountains, rivers, and seas. Mountains are described as anchors that stabilize the earth, preventing it from shaking. Rivers provide sustenance and water for life, while seas serve as a source of food and transport. The Quran emphasizes the precision and balance in the creation of the earth, highlighting Allah's wisdom and power. The earth is also considered a test for humanity, a place where they are given the opportunity to prove their faith and obedience to Allah.

The formation of the heavens and the earth is not only a physical event but also a spiritual one. The heavens represent the realm of the divine, while the earth represents the realm of humanity. The connection between the two is maintained through worship, prayer, and good deeds. The creation of the heavens and the earth serves as a reminder of Allah's greatness and His role as the ultimate creator and sustainer of the universe. It also emphasizes the importance of appreciating and preserving the natural world, recognizing it as a sign of Allah's blessings.

extendedContent.title: The Creation of Adam and the Significance of Humankind
extendedContent.content:
The creation of Adam (peace be upon him) marks a pivotal moment in the Islamic creation narrative. Allah created Adam from clay (tin) and breathed His spirit into him, endowing him with intellect, knowledge, and the capacity for moral reasoning. The Quran describes how Allah taught Adam the names of all things, granting him a unique understanding of the world. This act signifies the elevated status of humankind in the divine plan. The angels were then commanded to prostrate before Adam, acknowledging his superiority. This command was obeyed by all except Iblis (Satan), who refused out of pride and arrogance, leading to his eternal condemnation.

The creation of Adam is not merely a story of origin but also a symbol of human potential and responsibility. As Allah's vicegerent (khalifa) on Earth, humankind is entrusted with the care and stewardship of the planet. This role requires knowledge, wisdom, and a commitment to justice and righteousness. The story of Adam and Eve in paradise and their subsequent fall due to temptation serves as a cautionary tale, highlighting the importance of resisting evil and adhering to divine guidance. Their expulsion from paradise and their subsequent repentance demonstrate the possibility of redemption and the importance of seeking forgiveness.

The creation of humankind is considered the culmination of Allah's creative efforts, a testament to His infinite wisdom and mercy. Humans are endowed with the capacity to know and worship Allah, to create and innovate, and to build a just and compassionate society. This potential comes with a great responsibility to use these gifts wisely and to strive for excellence in all endeavors, always remembering that they are accountable to Allah for their actions. The story of Adam and the creation of humankind serves as a constant reminder of our purpose and our place in the universe.

extendedContent.title: The Tablet Preserved (Al-Lawh Al-Mahfuz) and Divine Decree
extendedContent.content:
A crucial element of the Creation Order is the existence of the Tablet Preserved (Al-Lawh Al-Mahfuz), a celestial record containing all that has been, is, and will be. This tablet is mentioned in the Quran (Surah Al-Buruj, 85:22) and is understood as a manifestation of Allah's complete knowledge and pre-ordained decree (Qadar). The existence of the Tablet Preserved signifies that everything in the universe operates according to a divine plan, even if the full extent of this plan is beyond human comprehension. The details inscribed on the tablet include the birth, death, and sustenance of every creature, as well as all the events that will occur throughout history.

The concept of the Tablet Preserved is closely linked to the Islamic understanding of free will and predestination. While everything is pre-ordained and recorded on the tablet, humans are still held accountable for their actions. This apparent paradox is a complex theological issue that has been debated by scholars for centuries. One interpretation is that Allah knows in advance what choices humans will make, but that these choices are still made freely. Another perspective suggests that the Tablet Preserved represents Allah's knowledge of all possibilities, and that human actions contribute to the unfolding of events within the framework of divine decree. Understanding the relationship between free will and predestination requires acknowledging the limitations of human understanding in grasping the infinite wisdom of Allah.

The belief in the Tablet Preserved encourages a sense of acceptance and trust in Allah's plan. It reminds believers that even in the face of hardship and uncertainty, there is a divine purpose behind everything. This belief can provide comfort and strength, allowing individuals to navigate the challenges of life with faith and resilience. The concept of the Tablet Preserved also underscores the importance of striving for good and avoiding evil, knowing that every action has consequences and contributes to the unfolding of Allah's divine plan. It serves as a reminder that humans are not merely puppets of fate but active participants in the grand narrative of creation.
